text: The surgical team begins the procedure by placing ports for robotic-assisted laparoscopic surgery. The patient is positioned in a modified dorsal lithotomy steep Trendelenburg position, with the robotic system positioned between the patient's legs. Maryland bipolar forceps are placed in the left robotic arm, monopolar scissors in the right arm, and tenaculum forceps in the third arm. The sigmoid colon is visible below, with the outline of the bladder ahead.

The surgeon makes an incision lateral to the left medial umbilical ligament, followed by a similar incision on the right side. After completing both incisions, the medial umbilical ligaments and the urachus are transected.

The surgeon develops the space of Retzius using a combination of blunt dissection and cautery, progressing until the pubic bone is encountered. The bladder and prostate are visible in the surgical field. The Foley catheter is manipulated to aid in identifying the bladder neck.

The surgeon opens the bladder transversely approximately three centimeters above the bladder neck using monopolar cautery. The Foley catheter balloon becomes visible, and tenaculum forceps are used to retract the anterior lip of the bladder wall. The dissection continues until reaching the lateral limits of the prostate.
The surgeon encounters bladder stones and removes them using a specimen retrieval bag inserted through the eleven millimeter assistant port. Five bladder stones are successfully extracted.

The surgeon uses a tenaculum to identify the posterior bladder neck and begins dissection by entering the plane between the prostate adenoma and the prostatic capsule. Bipolar and monopolar cautery are used to control bleeding, while blunt dissection and cautery extend the dissection plane circumferentially. The prostatic adenoma visibly separates from the capsule with the aid of the tenaculum for retraction.

The surgeon moves the dissection anteriorly after reaching the posterior and lateral limits. A zero bicoral suture on a CT needle is placed on the anterior bladder as a holding stitch, which is brought through the anterior abdominal wall using a fascial closure device. The dissection continues anteriorly around the prostate, with the adenoma nearly free. Once completely freed, the adenoma is placed in a specimen retrieval bag and removed in two pieces.

Microsutures are applied to control the pedicles at the five and seven o'clock positions. The urethra is approximated to the trigone of the bladder using a surgical slip knot with a two-oh microsuture. Five interrupted two-oh microsutures are placed. The urethral stents are removed, and hemostatic agents are applied as needed. A twenty-two French three-way Foley catheter is inserted. The bladder is closed in two layers using running two-oh Vicryl sutures. The first layer is demonstrated, followed by filling the bladder with two hundred cc's of normal saline to check for leaks, and the second layer is completed.

The surgeon places a closed suction device and removes all trocars under laparoscopic visualization. The assistant port is closed if necessary, and the specimen is removed. The skin and fascia are then closed.
